comment_text.sn42.13:1.1: The Koliyans were south-eastern neighbors of the Sakyans, with whom they had close ties in marriage and customs.
comment_text.sn42.13:1.2: For derivation of this name, see note on DN 16:1.19.2.
comment_text.sn42.13:1.4: Māyā means “illusion, trick, deceit”. Perhaps a reference to the “conversion trick” of which the Buddha was accused at MN 56:8.3 and AN 4.193:15.8. In his reply, the Buddha employs a careful logical method to demonstrate that his teaching is based on reason, not trickery.
comment_text.sn42.13:3.8: It seems that police corruption is not new.
comment_text.sn42.13:5.2: This uses the emphatic distributive form as at SN 42.8:1.5.
comment_text.sn42.13:6.3: Ambho is not usually used by itself as a vocative, but rather as an interjection. This would work for this sentence (“Oh my, what did that man do?”) but not for the response below. It seems a vocative sense is demanded; perhaps we should read ambho purisa, ayaṁ puriso.
comment_text.sn42.13:15.5: The ascetic teachers are normally located in Magadha, but here we see that, like the Buddha, the wandered extensively.
comment_text.sn42.13:16.1: Attributed to Ajita of the hair blanket at DN 2:23.2.
comment_text.sn42.13:22.1: Dhammasamādhi (“immersion based on understanding of principle”) is a unique term, and appears to have caused some confusion. The commentary offers various explanations (see Bodhi’s note in Connected Discourses). MA 20 has 有法之定，名曰遠離, translated by Analayo/Bucknell as “there is a Dharma meditation called abandoning.” The context, however, makes the sense clear. Samādhi has its usual meaning of “immersion”. Dhamma means the “principle” of cause and effect, as in “observation of principles” (dhammānupassanā), “investigation of principles” (dhammavicaya), or “discernment of principles” (dhammavipassanā). This discourse describes how a meditator who has already entered absorption can then use wisdom to reflect on the principles of causality, which gives rise to joy and further immersion.
comment_text.sn42.13:22.2: Tatra ce does not mean “if in that” (meditation state), but “if regarding that, if in that case”, and may often be translated simply as “if” (eg. AN 6.64:12.1, MN 22:38.1, Pli Tv Bu Pm:41.3).
comment_text.sn42.13:23.5: Compare MN 60, which likewise uses a careful conditional logic, while employing the language of gambling to assess outcomes after death.
comment_text.sn42.13:23.6: “Winning hand” (kaṭaggaha) is an expression from the classical Vedic game of chance. A large quantity of vibhītaka (“bedda”) nuts were placed in a hollow, from which players would take a handful. If the number of nuts was divisible by four, it was said to be a “perfect” (kaṭa) “hand” (gāha).

root_text.sn42.13:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā koliyesu viharati uttaraṁ nāma koliyānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn42.13:1.2: Atha kho pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn42.13:2.1: Ye te, bhante, evamāhaṁsu: ‘samaṇo gotamo māyaṁ jānātī’ti, kacci te, bhante, bhagavato vuttavādino, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkhanti, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākaronti, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānuvādo gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgacchati?
root_text.sn42.13:2.3: “Ye te, gāmaṇi, evamāhaṁsu: ‘samaṇo gotamo māyaṁ jānātī’ti, vuttavādino ceva me, te na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkhanti, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākaronti, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānuvādo gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgacchatī”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:2.4: “Saccaṁyeva kira, bho, mayaṁ tesaṁ samaṇabrāhmaṇānaṁ na saddahāma:
root_text.sn42.13:2.5: ‘samaṇo gotamo māyaṁ jānātī’ti, samaṇo khalu bho gotamo māyāvī”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:2.6: “Yo nu kho, gāmaṇi, evaṁ vadeti: ‘ahaṁ māyaṁ jānāmī’ti, so evaṁ vadeti: ‘ahaṁ māyāvī’”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:2.7: “Tatheva taṁ bhagavā hoti, tatheva taṁ sugata hotī”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:2.8: “Tena hi, gāmaṇi, taññevettha paṭipucchissāmi; yathā te khameyya, tathā taṁ byākareyyāsi—
root_text.sn42.13:3.4: “Taṁ kiṁ maññasi, gāmaṇi, kimatthiyā koliyānaṁ lambacūḷakā bhaṭā”ti?
root_text.sn42.13:3.5: “Ye ca, bhante, koliyānaṁ corā te ca paṭisedhetuṁ, yāni ca koliyānaṁ dūteyyāni tāni ca vahātuṁ, etadatthiyā, bhante, koliyānaṁ lambacūḷakā bhaṭā”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:3.7: jānāsi tvaṁ koliyānaṁ lambacūḷake bhaṭe sīlavante vā te dussīle vā”ti?
root_text.sn42.13:3.8: “Jānāmahaṁ, bhante, koliyānaṁ lambacūḷake bhaṭe dussīle pāpadhamme; ye ca loke dussīlā pāpadhammā koliyānaṁ lambacūḷakā bhaṭā tesaṁ aññatarā”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:3.10: ‘pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi jānāti koliyānaṁ lambacūḷake bhaṭe dussīle pāpadhamme, pāṭaliyopi gāmaṇi dussīlo pāpadhammo’ti, sammā nu kho so vadamāno vadeyyā”ti?
root_text.sn42.13:3.12: Aññe, bhante, koliyānaṁ lambacūḷakā bhaṭā, aññohamasmi. Aññathādhammā koliyānaṁ lambacūḷakā bhaṭā, aññathādhammohamasmī”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:3.13: “Tvañhi nāma, gāmaṇi, lacchasi: ‘pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi jānāti koliyānaṁ lambacūḷake bhaṭe dussīle pāpadhamme, na ca pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi dussīlo pāpadhammo’ti, kasmā tathāgato na lacchati: ‘tathāgato māyaṁ jānāti, na ca tathāgato māyāvī’ti?
root_text.sn42.13:3.14: Māyañcāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, pajānāmi, māyāya ca vipākaṁ, yathāpaṭipanno ca māyāvī kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā apāyaṁ duggatiṁ vinipātaṁ nirayaṁ upapajjati tañca pajānāmi.
root_text.sn42.13:4.1: Pāṇātipātañcāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, pajānāmi, pāṇātipātassa ca vipākaṁ, yathāpaṭipanno ca pāṇātipātī kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā apāyaṁ duggatiṁ vinipātaṁ nirayaṁ upapajjati tañca pajānāmi.

root_text.sn42.13:4.3: Kāmesumicchācārañcāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, pajānāmi, kāmesumicchācārassa ca vipākaṁ, yathāpaṭipanno ca kāmesumicchācārī kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā apāyaṁ duggatiṁ vinipātaṁ nirayaṁ upapajjati tañca pajānāmi.

root_text.sn42.13:4.10: Micchādiṭṭhiñcāhaṁ, gāmaṇi, pajānāmi, micchādiṭṭhiyā ca vipākaṁ, yathāpaṭipanno ca micchādiṭṭhiko kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā apāyaṁ duggatiṁ vinipātaṁ nirayaṁ upapajjati tañca pajānāmi.
root_text.sn42.13:5.1: Santi hi, gāmaṇi, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā evaṁvādino evaṁdiṭṭhino:
root_text.sn42.13:5.2: ‘yo koci pāṇamatipāteti, sabbo so diṭṭheva dhamme dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvedayati.
root_text.sn42.13:5.3: Yo koci adinnaṁ ādiyati, sabbo so diṭṭheva dhamme dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvedayati.
root_text.sn42.13:5.4: Yo koci kāmesu micchā carati, sabbo so diṭṭheva dhamme dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvedayati.
root_text.sn42.13:5.5: Yo koci musā bhaṇati, sabbo so diṭṭheva dhamme dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvedayatī’ti.
root_text.sn42.13:6.1: Dissati kho pana, gāmaṇi, idhekacco mālī kuṇḍalī sunhāto suvilitto kappitakesamassu itthikāmehi rājā maññe paricārento.

root_text.sn42.13:18.3: Khurapariyantena cepi cakkena yo imissā pathaviyā pāṇe ekaṁ maṁsakhalaṁ ekaṁ maṁsapuñjaṁ kareyya, natthi tatonidānaṁ pāpaṁ, natthi pāpassa āgamo.
root_text.sn42.13:18.4: Dakkhiṇañcepi gaṅgāya tīraṁ gaccheyya hananto ghātento chindanto chedāpento pacanto pācāpento, natthi tatonidānaṁ pāpaṁ, natthi pāpassa āgamo.
root_text.sn42.13:18.5: Uttarañcepi gaṅgāya tīraṁ gaccheyya dadanto dāpento yajanto yajāpento, natthi tatonidānaṁ puññaṁ, natthi puññassa āgamo.

root_text.sn42.13:20.1: Tassa mayhaṁ, bhante, ahudeva kaṅkhā, ahu vicikicchā:
root_text.sn42.13:20.2: ‘kosu nāma imesaṁ bhavataṁ samaṇabrāhmaṇānaṁ saccaṁ āha, ko musā’”ti?
root_text.sn42.13:21.2: Kaṅkhanīye ca pana te ṭhāne vicikicchā uppannā”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:21.3: “Evaṁ pasannohaṁ, bhante, bhagavati. Pahoti me bhagavā tathā dhammaṁ desetuṁ yathāhaṁ imaṁ kaṅkhādhammaṁ pajaheyyan”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:22.2: Tatra ce tvaṁ cittasamādhiṁ paṭilabheyyāsi. Evaṁ tvaṁ imaṁ kaṅkhādhammaṁ pajaheyyāsi.
root_text.sn42.13:22.4: Idha, gāmaṇi, ariyasāvako pāṇātipātaṁ pahāya pāṇātipātā paṭivirato hoti, adinnādānaṁ pahāya adinnādānā paṭivirato hoti, kāmesumicchācāraṁ pahāya kāmesumicchācārā paṭivirato hoti, musāvādaṁ pahāya musāvādā paṭivirato hoti, pisuṇaṁ vācaṁ pahāya pisuṇāya vācāya paṭivirato hoti, pharusaṁ vācaṁ pahāya pharusāya vācāya paṭivirato hoti, samphappalāpaṁ pahāya samphappalāpā paṭivirato hoti, abhijjhaṁ pahāya anabhijjhālu hoti, byāpādapadosaṁ pahāya abyāpannacitto hoti, micchādiṭṭhiṁ pahāya sammādiṭṭhiko hoti.
root_text.sn42.13:23.1: Sa kho so, gāmaṇi, ariyasāvako evaṁ vigatābhijjho vigatabyāpādo asammūḷho sampajāno paṭissato mettāsahagatena cetasā ekaṁ disaṁ pharitvā viharati, tathā dutiyaṁ, tathā tatiyaṁ, tathā catutthaṁ, iti uddhamadho tiriyaṁ sabbadhi sabbattatāya sabbāvantaṁ lokaṁ mettāsahagatena cetasā vipulena mahaggatena appamāṇena averena abyāpajjena pharitvā viharati.
root_text.sn42.13:23.4: “natthi dinnaṁ, natthi yiṭṭhaṁ, natthi hutaṁ, natthi sukatadukkaṭānaṁ kammānaṁ phalaṁ vipāko, natthi ayaṁ loko, natthi paro loko, natthi mātā, natthi pitā, natthi sattā opapātikā, natthi loke samaṇabrāhmaṇā, sammaggatā sammāpaṭipannā ye imañca lokaṁ parañca lokaṁ sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā pavedentī”ti.
root_text.sn42.13:23.5: Sace tassa bhoto satthuno saccaṁ vacanaṁ, apaṇṇakatāya mayhaṁ, yvāhaṁ na kiñci byābādhemi tasaṁ vā thāvaraṁ vā.
root_text.sn42.13:23.6: Ubhayamettha kaṭaggāho, yaṁ camhi kāyena saṁvuto vācāya saṁvuto manasā saṁvuto, yañca kāyassa bhedā paraṁ maraṇā sugatiṁ saggaṁ lokaṁ upapajjissāmī’ti.

root_text.sn42.13:32.1: Evaṁ vutte, pāṭaliyo gāmaṇi bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:

translation_text.sn42.13:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Koliyans, where they have a town named Uttarakā.
translation_text.sn42.13:1.2: Then Pāṭaliya the chief went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn42.13:2.1: Do those who say this repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? Is their explanation in line with the teaching? Are there any legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?
translation_text.sn42.13:2.2: For we don’t want to misrepresent the Blessed One.”
translation_text.sn42.13:2.3: “Chief, those who say this repeat what I have said, and don’t misrepresent me with an untruth. Their explanation is in line with the teaching, and there are no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.”
translation_text.sn42.13:2.4: “Sir, we didn’t believe that what those ascetics and brahmins said was really true.
translation_text.sn42.13:2.5: But it seems the ascetic Gotama is deceitful!”
translation_text.sn42.13:2.6: “Chief, does someone who says ‘I know deceit’ also say ‘I am deceitful’?”
translation_text.sn42.13:2.7: “That’s right, Blessed One! That’s right, Holy One!”
translation_text.sn42.13:2.8: “Well then, chief, I’ll ask you about this in return, and you can answer as you like.
translation_text.sn42.13:3.2: Do you know the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses?”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.5: “To put a stop to bandits and to deliver messages for the Koliyans.”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.7: Are the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses moral or immoral?”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.8: “I know that they’re immoral, of bad character, sir. They are among those in the world who are immoral and of bad character.”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.9: “Would it be right to say that
translation_text.sn42.13:3.10: Pāṭaliya knows the Kolyian officers with drooping headdresses who are immoral, of bad character, so he too must be immoral and of bad character?”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.12: I’m quite different from the Koliyan officers with drooping headdresses; we have quite different characters.”
translation_text.sn42.13:3.13: “So if you can know those officers of bad character while you are not of bad character, why can’t the Realized One know deceit, without being deceitful?
translation_text.sn42.13:3.14: I understand deceit and its result. And I understand how the deceitful practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.
translation_text.sn42.13:4.1: I understand killing living creatures and its result. And I understand how those who kill living creatures practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.
translation_text.sn42.13:4.10: wrong view and its result. And I understand how those who have wrong view practice so that when their body breaks up, after death, they’re reborn in a place of loss, a bad place, the underworld, hell.
translation_text.sn42.13:5.1: There are some ascetics and brahmins who have this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:5.2: ‘Everyone who kills living creatures experiences pain and sadness in this very life.
translation_text.sn42.13:5.5: lies experiences pain and sadness in this very life.’
translation_text.sn42.13:6.1: But you can see someone, garlanded and adorned, nicely bathed and anointed, hair and beard dressed, taking his pleasure with women as if he were a king.
translation_text.sn42.13:6.3: ‘My friend, what did that man do?’
translation_text.sn42.13:6.5: ‘My friend, that man attacked the king’s enemy and killed them.
translation_text.sn42.13:6.6: The king was delighted and gave him this reward.
translation_text.sn42.13:6.7: That’s why he’s garlanded and adorned, nicely bathed and anointed, hair and beard dressed, taking his pleasure with women as if he were a king.’
translation_text.sn42.13:7.1: And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back with a strong rope. His head is shaven and he’s marched from street to street and from square to square to the beating of a harsh drum. Then he’s taken out the south gate and there, to the south of the city, they chop off his head.
translation_text.sn42.13:7.3: ‘My friend, what did that man do?’
translation_text.sn42.13:7.5: ‘My friend, that man is an enemy of the king, and he has murdered a man or a woman.
translation_text.sn42.13:7.6: That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’
translation_text.sn42.13:8.2: Have you seen or heard of such a thing?”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.3: “Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.4: “Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that
translation_text.sn42.13:8.5: everyone who kills living creatures experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong?”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.7: “But those who speak hollow, false nonsense: are they moral or immoral?”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.9: “And are those who are immoral, of bad character practicing wrongly or rightly?”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.11: “And do those who are practicing wrongly have wrong view or right view?”
translation_text.sn42.13:8.13: “But is it appropriate to have confidence in those of wrong view?”
translation_text.sn42.13:9.1: “You can see someone, garlanded and adorned …
translation_text.sn42.13:9.5: ‘My friend, that man attacked the king’s enemy and took their valuables.
translation_text.sn42.13:9.6: The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ …
translation_text.sn42.13:10.1: And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back …
translation_text.sn42.13:10.4: ‘My friend, that man took something from a village or wilderness, with the intention to commit theft.
translation_text.sn42.13:10.5: That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’
translation_text.sn42.13:10.7: Have you seen or heard of such a thing?”
translation_text.sn42.13:10.8: “Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.”
translation_text.sn42.13:10.9: “Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that
translation_text.sn42.13:10.10: everyone who steals experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong? …
translation_text.sn42.13:10.11: Is it appropriate to have confidence in them?”
translation_text.sn42.13:11.1: “You can see someone, garlanded and adorned …
translation_text.sn42.13:11.5: ‘My friend, that man had sexual relations with the wives of an enemy king.
translation_text.sn42.13:11.6: The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ …
translation_text.sn42.13:12.1: And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back …
translation_text.sn42.13:12.5: ‘My friend, that man had sexual relations with the women and maidens of good families.
translation_text.sn42.13:12.6: That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’
translation_text.sn42.13:12.8: Have you seen or heard of such a thing?”
translation_text.sn42.13:12.9: “Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.”
translation_text.sn42.13:12.10: “Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that
translation_text.sn42.13:12.11: everyone who commits sexual misconduct experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong? …
translation_text.sn42.13:12.12: Is it appropriate to have confidence in them?”
translation_text.sn42.13:13.1: “And you can see someone, garlanded and adorned …
translation_text.sn42.13:13.5: ‘My friend, that man amused the king with lies.
translation_text.sn42.13:13.6: The king was delighted and gave him this reward. …’ …
translation_text.sn42.13:14.1: And you can see someone else, his arms tied tightly behind his back …
translation_text.sn42.13:14.5: ‘My friend, that man has ruined a householder or householder’s child by lying.
translation_text.sn42.13:14.6: That’s why the rulers arrested him and inflicted such punishment.’
translation_text.sn42.13:14.8: Have you seen or heard of such a thing?”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.9: “Sir, we have seen it and heard of it, and we will hear of it again.”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.10: “Since this is so, the ascetics and brahmins whose view is that
translation_text.sn42.13:14.11: everyone who lies experiences pain and sadness in this very life: are they right or wrong?”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.13: “But those who speak hollow, false nonsense: are they moral or immoral?”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.15: “And are those who are immoral, of bad character practicing wrongly or rightly?”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.17: “And do those who are practicing wrongly have wrong view or right view?”
translation_text.sn42.13:14.19: “But is it appropriate to have confidence in those of wrong view?”
translation_text.sn42.13:15.3: where there are cots, seats, water jars, and oil lamps.
translation_text.sn42.13:15.4: Whenever an ascetic or brahmin comes to stay, I share what I have as best I can.
translation_text.sn42.13:15.5: Once it so happened, sir, that four teachers of different views, beliefs, and opinions came to stay at my guest house.
translation_text.sn42.13:16.1: One teacher had this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:16.2: ‘There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’
translation_text.sn42.13:17.1: One teacher had this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:17.2: ‘There is meaning in giving, sacrifice, and offerings. There are fruits and results of good and bad deeds. There is an afterlife. There are such things as mother and father, and beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there are ascetics and brahmins who are rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describe the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.’
translation_text.sn42.13:18.1: One teacher had this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:18.2: ‘The one who acts does nothing wrong when they punish, mutilate, torture, aggrieve, oppress, intimidate, or when they encourage others to do the same. Nothing bad is done when they kill, steal, break into houses, plunder wealth, steal from isolated buildings, commit highway robbery, commit adultery, and lie.
translation_text.sn42.13:18.3: If you were to reduce all the living creatures of this earth to one heap and mass of flesh with a razor-edged chakram, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil.
translation_text.sn42.13:18.4: If you were to go along the south bank of the Ganges killing, mutilating, and torturing, and encouraging others to do the same, no evil comes of that, and no outcome of evil.
translation_text.sn42.13:18.5: If you were to go along the north bank of the Ganges giving and sacrificing and encouraging others to do the same, no merit comes of that, and no outcome of merit.
translation_text.sn42.13:18.6: In giving, self-control, restraint, and truthfulness there is no merit or outcome of merit.’
translation_text.sn42.13:19.1: One teacher had this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:19.2: ‘The one who acts does a bad deed when they punish, mutilate, torture, aggrieve, oppress, intimidate, or when they encourage others to do the same. A bad deed is done when they kill, steal, break into houses, plunder wealth, steal from isolated buildings, commit highway robbery, commit adultery, and lie.
translation_text.sn42.13:19.3: If you were to reduce all the living creatures of this earth to one heap and mass of flesh with a razor-edged chakram, evil comes of that, and an outcome of evil.
translation_text.sn42.13:19.4: If you were to go along the south bank of the Ganges killing, mutilating, and torturing, and encouraging others to do the same, evil comes of that, and an outcome of evil.
translation_text.sn42.13:19.5: If you were to go along the north bank of the Ganges giving and sacrificing and encouraging others to do the same, merit comes of that, and an outcome of merit.
translation_text.sn42.13:19.6: In giving, self-control, restraint, and truthfulness there is merit and outcome of merit.’
translation_text.sn42.13:20.1: I had doubt and uncertainty about that:
translation_text.sn42.13:20.2: ‘I wonder who of these respected ascetics and brahmins speaks the truth, and who speaks falsehood?’”
translation_text.sn42.13:21.1: “Chief, no wonder you’re doubting and uncertain.
translation_text.sn42.13:21.2: Doubt has come up in you about an uncertain matter.”
translation_text.sn42.13:21.3: “I am quite confident that the Buddha is capable of teaching me so that I can give up this state of uncertainty.”
translation_text.sn42.13:22.1: “Chief, there is immersion based on understanding of principle.
translation_text.sn42.13:22.2: If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty.
translation_text.sn42.13:22.3: And what is immersion based on understanding of principle?
translation_text.sn42.13:22.4: It’s when a noble disciple has given up killing living creatures, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, divisive speech, harsh speech, talking nonsense, covetousness, ill will, and wrong view.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.1: Then that noble disciple is rid of desire, rid of ill will, unconfused, aware, and mindful. They meditate spreading a heart full of love to one direction, and to the second, and to the third, and to the fourth. In the same way above, below, across, everywhere, all around, they spread a heart full of love to the whole world—abundant, expansive, limitless, free of enmity and ill will.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.3: ‘That teacher who had this doctrine and view:
translation_text.sn42.13:23.4: “There’s no meaning in giving, sacrifice, or offerings. There’s no fruit or result of good and bad deeds. There’s no afterlife. There’s no such thing as mother and father, or beings that are reborn spontaneously. And there’s no ascetic or brahmin who is rightly comported and rightly practiced, and who describes the afterlife after realizing it with their own insight.”
translation_text.sn42.13:23.5: If what this good teacher says is true, it’s a sure bet for me to not hurt any creature firm or frail.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.6: I hold a winning hand on both counts, since I’m restrained in body, speech, and mind, and when my body breaks up, after death, I’ll be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.’
translation_text.sn42.13:23.9: When the mind is full of rapture, the body becomes tranquil.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.10: When the body is tranquil, they feel bliss.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.11: And when blissful, the mind becomes immersed in samādhi.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.12: This is that immersion based on understanding of principle.
translation_text.sn42.13:23.13: If you gain such mental immersion, you can give up that state of uncertainty.

translation_text.sn42.13:32.1: When he said this, Pāṭaliya the chief said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn42.13:32.3: From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”
translation_text.sn42.13:33.5: The Linked Discourses on chiefs are complete.
